g.text: the network outputs the noise eps-hat
x_0 = (x_s - s.eps-hat)/(1-s) weak signal near s=1 (target ~
the network outputs the clean image x_0-hat
the network outputs v-hat = eps - x_0
x_0 = x_s - s.v-hat , eps = x_s + (1-s).v-hat balanced
across all s -> SD3 / Wan / DF
x_sigma = (1 - sigma) x_0 + sigma . eps (here sigma = 0.4)
small sigma -> mostly clean, large sigma -> mostly noise
v-pred: output the velocity v = eps - x_0 (the line's direction, data -> noise)
Same x_sigma, same network — only the OUTPUT TARGET (and its loss weight) changes. Convert freely with the formulas above. v-pred is the modern default because its
target stays informative at every noise level.
Given x_sigma and sigma, each determines the other two — so they are interchangeable; they differ in loss weighting and stability across noise levels.

At noise level sigma, the noised image is a blend of the clean image x_0 and the noise eps. The network can output the noise (eps-pred), the clean image (x_0-pred), or the velocity v = eps - x_0 (v-pred).
What does the denoiser predict? — eps, x_0, or v (three equivalent targets)
